mappings.rationaleAI: The nervous system article directly covers the role of the nervous system in receiving sensory information, processing it in the brain and spinal cord, and initiating appropriate behavioral responses — the precise content of outline path C.3.

The brain article covers sensory integration, motor coordination, homeostasis, and behavioral regulation, including the prefrontal cortex's role in executive function — core to how the nervous system processes information and initiates behaviour per C.3.

Animal covers the full diversity of animal kingdom behaviours, body plans, and evolutionary history, supporting both A.1 (variety of animal behaviour) and providing the taxonomic scope needed for A.2 (classification of animal behaviour by group).

Learning directly addresses the influence of experience on behaviour, covering habituation, sensitization, classical and operant conditioning, and observational learning — phyletic patterns in the evolution of learning that are the explicit subject of D.2.

Heredity's coverage of genetic transmission across generations and Mendelian laws provides the conceptual foundation for D.1 (evidence of the genetic determination of behaviour), connecting inherited traits to behavioural phenotypes.

Genetics addresses the genetic determination of behaviour through its coverage of gene-environment interaction (nature-nurture), population genetics, and gene regulation, directly supporting outline path D.1 on evidence for the genetic determination of behaviour.

Human behavior covers the variety of behavioral responses (A.1), the classification of behavior by domain (cognitive, social, moral, developmental — A.2), and the components shaping behavior (genetic, physiological, environmental — A.3), directly addressing the priority paths A.2 and A.3.

Zoology explicitly covers animal behaviour (ethology), classification of animal diversity, and the scientific study of all animal taxa — providing content for the variety (A.1) and classification (A.2) of animal behaviour as priority paths.

Botany covers plant anatomy, physiology, and function including plant responses to environmental stimuli — directly relevant to B.1 (plant movements including tropic and nastic movements), the only outline path specifically about plant behaviour.

Insects exhibit diverse fixed action patterns, instinctive behaviours, and reflexive responses (B.2), and their extraordinary behavioural diversity — from eusocial organisation to metamorphosis — contributes to A.1 (variety of animal behaviour).

Birds exhibit complex and diverse behaviours including cultural transmission, diverse mating systems, and sophisticated social behaviour (A.1); their learned behaviours (song learning) represent key examples of phyletic patterns in the evolution of learning (D.2).

Sex covers sex-determination systems and sexual dimorphism arising from sexual selection and differential selective pressures — directly relevant to C.2 (hormonal influences on behaviour, including sex hormones shaping reproductive behaviour and dimorphism).

Human sexuality explicitly covers the sexual response cycle, hormonal influences on sexual behaviour, and the role of prenatal hormonal environment in determining sexual orientation — directly mapping to C.2 (hormonal influences on behaviour, including sex hormones).

Sexual orientation covers genetic factors and prenatal hormone exposure as biological determinants of enduring behavioural patterns — evidence for the genetic determination of behaviour (D.1) and hormonal influences on behaviour (C.2).

Gender addresses hormonal and neurological influences on behavioral and psychological dimensions of sex, including the role of sex hormones in shaping behavior — relevant to C.2 (hormonal influences on behaviour by sex hormones).

Addiction involves neurobiological changes in the mesocorticolimbic dopamine-glutamate reward pathway, directly addressing the interaction of endocrine and nervous systems (C.1) and hormonal influences on compulsive behaviour (C.2).

Memory is central to how the nervous system stores and retrieves information processed in the brain — directly supporting C.3's focus on the nervous system's role in receiving, processing, and responding to information.

Emotion involves neurophysiological substrates (prefrontal cortex, limbic system) and neuroendocrine influences, covering the interaction of nervous and endocrine systems (C.1) and the role of hormones in behavioral and emotional states (C.2).

Communication covers animal, plant, fungal, and bacterial signaling as components of behavioural interaction — directly relevant to A.3 (components of animal behaviour, a priority path), which includes communication as a key behavioral component.

The domestic cat article covers pheromone-based communication, ambush predation instincts, crepuscular behavioural patterns, and behavioural adaptability — illustrating stereotyped instinctive responses (B.2) and contributing to the variety of animal behaviour (A.1).

Dogs show genetically heritable behavioural traits selected through domestication (D.1 — genetic determination of behaviour), and their communication integrating visual, vocal, postural, and chemical signals illustrates the variety of animal behavioural components (A.1).
